metadata.career_relevance: Zerodha processes 10M+ trades daily. The trade execution write path (buy/sell orders) is highly transactional and must be strongly consistent. The portfolio view query path (what is my P&L right now?) is read-heavy and can be eventually consistent. CQRS separates these: write DB optimized for transactions, read DB optimized for fast portfolio dashboard queries. After this topic, you can design systems that scale reads and writes independently.

hook.real_world_problem:
Zerodha portfolio dashboard:

```
SAME MODEL FOR READS AND WRITES (problem):
  Trade entity: join trades + positions + instruments + prices
  Write: INSERT trade (fast, simple)
  Read (portfolio view): JOIN 5 tables, calculate P&L, aggregate positions
  → Dashboard query: 800ms (too slow for real-time trading)
  → Write path: slowed by index maintenance on read-optimized columns

With CQRS:
  WRITE SIDE: TradeCommand → TradingDB (normalized, transactional)
  READ SIDE: TradeProjection → PortfolioDB (denormalized, pre-calculated)
  → Dashboard query: 15ms (pre-aggregated portfolio view)
  → Write path: no join overhead (write to normalized trade table only)
  → Scale independently: 100 pods for read, 10 pods for write
```
hook.what_you_will_build: CQRS trade system: command side (place order) and query side (portfolio view) with separate models.

concept_explanation.cqrs_overview:
**CQRS — separate read and write models:**

```
COMMAND SIDE (Write):
  Commands: PlaceOrderCommand, CancelOrderCommand
  Command handlers: validate + execute + emit events
  Write store: normalized PostgreSQL (strong consistency)
  Optimized for: data integrity, ACID transactions

QUERY SIDE (Read):
  Queries: GetPortfolioQuery, GetTradeHistoryQuery
  Query handlers: read from denormalized read model
  Read store: optimized read DB or Redis (eventual consistency)
  Optimized for: fast reads, reporting, dashboards

SYNC MECHANISM:
  Command side emits events (OrderPlaced, OrderFilled)
  Projection handler consumes events → updates read model
  Eventual consistency: read model catches up after brief delay
```

visual_aid.description: CQRS architecture diagram. Left side: Command flow — Client → Command (PlaceOrder) → CommandHandler → Write DB (normalized). Right side: Query flow — Client → Query (GetPortfolio) → QueryHandler → Read DB (denormalized portfolio view). Center: Event bus connecting write to read (OrderPlaced event → Projection → updates Read DB). Annotations: Write DB: strong consistency, ACID. Read DB: eventual consistency, fast reads, pre-aggregated.
visual_aid.alt_text: CQRS command and query separation with event-driven projection

worked_example.scenario: Zerodha order service: CQRS with PlaceOrderCommand and PortfolioView query.
worked_example.problem_statement:
1. PlaceOrderCommand record + OrderCommandHandler
2. GetPortfolioQuery + PortfolioQueryHandler
3. PortfolioProjection: updates read model on OrderFilledEvent
4. @SpringBootTest: command places order, query returns portfolio

step_by_step_solution.details: CQRS adds complexity: two models, eventual consistency, projection maintenance. Use ONLY when read/write ratio is very skewed (10:1 or higher), or when read and write optimizations conflict. Simple CRUD apps: don't use CQRS. Portfolio dashboard (1000 reads per 1 write): great fit for CQRS.

worked_example.final_code:
// Command → CommandHandler → Write DB → Event → Projection → Read DB
// Query → QueryHandler → Read DB (fast, no joins needed)
things_to_notice: Eventual consistency: after PlaceOrderCommand returns, the portfolio query may briefly show the old state (before the projection processes the event). This is expected and acceptable for a dashboard.
No joins in query handler: the read model is pre-computed (denormalized). PortfolioView contains merchantId, totalValue, positions — all pre-aggregated by the projection.
Command returns only an ID (not the full state): commands are fire-and-forget. The write side says 'accepted', not 'here is your current state'. Use a query to get state after a command.
Separate scaling: read service (PortfolioQueryHandler) can be scaled to 50 pods for peak dashboard traffic. Write service (OrderCommandHandler) at 5 pods. Independent horizontal scaling.

industry_examples.use_case: Write side: order execution (PostgreSQL, ACID). Read side: Kite dashboard portfolio view (Redis-backed materialized view, pre-calculated P&L). Event: OrderFilled → Projection → update Redis portfolio. Dashboard loads in 20ms (was 2s with JOIN). During Diwali trading peak: read pods scale to 200, write pods stay at 20.

applications.interview_relevance: 'When would you use CQRS?' → When read and write operations have very different performance requirements. Portfolio dashboard: millions of reads but far fewer trades. Reads need denormalized views for speed; writes need normalized data for integrity. CQRS lets you optimize each independently. Trade-off: eventual consistency (brief delay between write and read model sync). Not for: simple CRUD, small apps where complexity cost > benefit.
applications.real_world_workflow: CQRS workflow: (1) define commands and queries as separate types, (2) command handlers: validate + persist + emit event, (3) query handlers: read from optimized read model, (4) projection: consume events + update read model asynchronously, (5) monitor: projection lag (time between event and read model update).

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Command handler returning the full entity or domain model (e.g., return Order after PlaceOrderCommand). Commands should return only an ID or nothing — they represent an intent, not a query.
common_gaps.remediation: Commands: return OrderId (or void). State: use a query after the command. This enforces the separation and prevents building half-CQRS systems where the command side is also doing read-side work.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: @PostMapping returns the full entity after a command — read data from command handler bypasses the read model

key_takeaways: CQRS: separate read and write models — each optimized independently
Command: intent to change state → CommandHandler → emit event
Query: request for data → QueryHandler → read from pre-built read model
Projection: event listener that updates read model asynchronously
Eventual consistency: read model may lag briefly behind write model
Scale reads and writes independently: 10× read pods vs write pods
Use when: read/write ratio very skewed OR read/write optimizations conflict
Don't use when: simple CRUD or small apps (complexity not worth it)

self_check_questions.question: What is 'eventual consistency' in CQRS and is it acceptable for a trading portfolio dashboard?
self_check_questions.answer: Eventual consistency: after a trade is placed (command), the portfolio view (read model) may briefly show the old state before the projection processes the OrderFilledEvent and updates the view. This delay is typically milliseconds to seconds depending on event processing speed. For a portfolio dashboard: acceptable. Users view their portfolio after the trade is placed — the dashboard showing the old value for 100ms is imperceptible. Not acceptable for: trade execution itself (must be strongly consistent — can't allow overselling), payment confirmation (must be immediately accurate). Rule: use eventual consistency for views and reporting; use strong consistency for the transactional write path.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Next: Event Sourcing — instead of storing only the current state, store every event that led to that state. The database becomes a complete audit trail.
